summary: This advice column covers several topics: a reader grappling with their implicit bias after being surprised that a math blogger was an African-American woman, another seeking advice on managing frequent crushes while in a committed relationship, a brief exchange on sex tourism and legalized prostitution, and thoughts on gender segregation in sports. Aunt Pythia advises the first reader to openly admit their prejudice as a first step toward addressing it, and encourages the second to embrace their crushes rather than suppress them. The column also critiques American football as a modern gladiator contest where poor minority men sacrifice their bodies for entertainment.
key_points: Everyone carries implicit biases, and acknowledging prejudice is the essential first step toward addressing it
Suppressing emotions like crushes does 'weird things' - it's healthier to experience them fully while trusting yourself not to act destructively
Legalized prostitution in impoverished countries isn't truly a 'chosen profession' when driven by dire economic need
American football has become a sport where poor minority men sacrifice their bodies while wealthier families opt out
Getting to know someone better usually diminishes rather than intensifies attraction - people are 'sexiest' when you know very little about them
money_quotes: Yes, you are prejudiced! We all are! I am too! It's an important part of growing up, admitting such things. We are flawed, and we are contributing to the problems of our culture. Fact.
You've got to live your life fully, and engage in the things that attract you, and trust yourself not to lose it entirely. You've really got no other options.
Almost nobody gets sexier when you get to know them better. People are at their very sexiest when you know about 10 minutes about them, scattered over a few weeks or months.
When you're an emo, it's all about enjoying the pain.
It's not really a 'chosen profession' if you are forced by dire need to do it.
American football is quickly becoming a sport to which poor minority men sacrifice their bodies.
We might as well just admit it's a modern day Gladiator Contest, used to maintain a culture of violence for a people convinced they must be warriors.
key_insight: Acknowledging our flaws and biases honestly - whether about prejudice or emotional patterns - is more productive than suppression or denial.
